revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in the Kita-ku ward of Tokyo, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōji Shrine
王子神社 | image = Oji jinja - Honden.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'' of Oji shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.7533|139.7359|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]] | founded_by = | established = 1320s | date_destroyed = | location = 1 Chome-1-12 Ōjihonchō, [[Kita, Tokyo|Kita-ku]], [[Tokyo]] [[〒]] 114-0022 | website = {{URL|http://ojijinja.tokyo.jp}} | architecture_style = [[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ōji Shrine'''|王子神社|Ōji-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the [[Kita, Tokyo|Kita ward]] of [[Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. Established during the [[Kamakura period]], most likely around 1321–1324, the shrine gives the name of "Ōji" to this area of the city. Most of the original buildings in the shrine precincts were destroyed during [[World War II]], and were rebuilt from the late 50s to 1982. It is notable for its giant [[Ginkgo biloba|ginkgo tree]],{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} suspected to be 600 years old and designated a [[List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|Natural Monument]] in 1939, and for its annual festival (held in August), which includes a [[mikoshi]] parade and a performance of [[dengaku]] dance.{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} It is one of the {{Nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}},{{cite web |url=http://tencoo.fc2web.com/jinja/xzyussha.htm |title=Tokyo Ten Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher= |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} of which it has been designated as the "northern protector shrine".{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == History == The exact date in which the Ōji Shrine was founded is not known, but according to a stone with inscriptions (designated a [[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)|Tangible Cultural Property]] of Tokyo) still standing beside the ginkgo tree, near the shrine precincts, Toyoshima contributed to found the shrine between 1321 and 1324.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The giant ginkgo tree is suspected to have been planted at around this time. [[Ōta Dōkan]] (1432-1486), the [[samurai]] and [[Buddhist monk]] who designed and built the [[Edo Castle]] (now the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]]), is said to have taken shelter from a storm under a big [[castanopsis]] tree in the shrine precincts which was destroyed, probably during [[World War II]].{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The area of the [[Kita, Tokyo|Kita-ku]] [[Special wards of Tokyo|ward]] surrounding it receives the name of "Ōji" from the shrine.{{cite web |url=http://dennisamith.com/2017/11/15/the-one-about-oji-jinja-shrine-in-kita/ |title=The One about Oji Jinja Shrine in Kita |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Dennis A. Amith |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == Architecture == Most of the original buildings of Ōji Shrine were destroyed in 1945 during [[World War II]], and were rebuilt in 1959-1964[[:ja:王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社 (東京都北区)]]{{Circular reference|date=August 2023}}{{Circular reference|date=November 2020}} and in 1982.{{cite web |url=http://dennisamith.com/2017/11/15/the-one-about-oji-jinja-shrine-in-kita/ |title=The One about Oji Jinja Shrine in Kita |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Dennis A. Amith |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} [[File:Oji jinja - Torii.jpg|thumb|left|''myōjin torii'' at the main entrance]] [[File:Oji jinja - Chōzuya.jpg|thumb|right|''Chōzuya'' at Ōji shrine]] [[File:Oji jinja - Honden 3.jpg|thumb|right|Interior of the ''Honden'']] [[File:Oji jinja - Seki Jinja 1.jpg|thumb|left|''Seki Jinja'' subsidiary shrine]] [[File:Oji jinja - Ginkgo tree 3.jpg|thumb|right|The giant ''ginkgo'' tree]] === Torii and Chōzuya === The shrine entrance is marked by a big concrete {{nihongo|''torii''|鳥居||literally bird abode}} in the [[Torii#Myōjin family|''myōjin'']] style, very common in [[Shinto architecture]], characterized by curved upper lintels. A short {{nihongo|''sandō''|参道||visiting path}} leads from the torii to the honden. On its right side stand a {{nihongo|''chōzuya''|手水舎|}}, the traditional water ablution pavilion used by worshippers to purify themselves before approaching the shrine. There is a back access to the shrine through a long stairway from the Otonashi Shinsui Park. This is where the giant ginkgo tree stands. === Honden === The main building of Ōji shrine is a {{nihongo|''[[honden]]''| 本殿|main hall}} in the ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]'' style, a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, the ''[[heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'', or offertory hall, and the ''honden'', are all interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on December 21, 2017 The [[kami]] worshipped here are [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] and [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]], considered to be "especially benevolent regarding marriage ceremonies, marital harmony, and restoring luck".{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} === Seki Shrine === At the left of the ''sandō'' stands a subsidiary shrine called ''Seki shrine''. This small shrine has been described as "rare even within Japan since it is dedicated to an ancestral deity in charge of hair."{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} === Ginkgo tree === The ''ginkgo biloba'' or ginkgo tree stands on a hill of the left bank of the Otonashi river, just beside the shrine precincts, at the right side of the sandō. It has a height of 24.2 m and a trunk circumference of 5.2 m. It is suspected to have been planted during the construction of the shrine, which would make it more than 600 years old.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} Unlike most of the Ōji shrine, the ginkgo tree survived the [[bombing of Tokyo]] during [[World War II]], although the top part of the trunk was damaged.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} One of the main attractions of the shrine, it was designated a [[List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|Natural Monument]] in 1939.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} Another giant ginkgo tree of similar characteristics in Tokyo is located in the grounds of [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]]. == Annual Events == The annual festival of Ōji Shrine is held the first Sunday of August.{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} It features a [[mikoshi]] parade, and the performance of the ''Ōji Jinja Dengakumai'' dance, one of Japan's three great [[dengaku]] dances.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == Access == There is no admission fee for visitors to enter the shrine precincts, and there is parking space available.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The entrances is at a 3-minute walk from the north exit of [[Ōji Station (Tokyo)|Ōji Station]] on the [[Keihin-Tōhoku Line]], or from exit 3 of the same station on the [[Tokyo Metro Namboku Line]], at a 5-minute walk from [[Ōji-ekimae Station]] and a 7-minute walk from [[Asukayama Station]] on the [[Toden Arakawa Line]].{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == References == {{reflist|2}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Oji jinja (Tokyo)|Oji Shrine}} *[http://ojijinja.tokyo.jp Official website] (in Japanese) {{Kita, Tokyo}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ōji Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]] {{Kumano Faith}}

precincts, Toyoshima contributed to found the shrine between 1321 and 1324.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The giant ginkgo tree is suspected to have been planted at around this time. [[Ōta Dōkan]] (1432-1486), the [[samurai]] and [[Buddhist monk]] who designed and built the [[Edo Castle]] (now the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]]), is said to have taken shelter from a storm under a big [[castanopsis]] tree in the shrine precincts which was destroyed, probably during [[World War II]].{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The area of the [[Kita, Tokyo|Kita-ku]] [[Special wards of Tokyo|ward]] surrounding it receives the name of "Ōji" from the shrine.{{cite web |url=http://dennisamith.com/2017/11/15/the-one-about-oji-jinja-shrine-in-kita/ |title=The One about Oji Jinja Shrine in Kita |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Dennis A. Amith |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == Architecture == Most of the original buildings of Ōji Shrine were destroyed in 1945 during [[World War II]], and were rebuilt in 1959-1964[[:ja:王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社 (東京都北区)]]{{Circular reference|date=August 2023}}{{Circular reference|date=November 2020}} and in 1982.{{cite web |url=http://dennisamith.com/2017/11/15/the-one-about-oji-jinja-shrine-in-kita/ |title=The One about Oji Jinja Shrine in Kita |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Dennis A. Amith |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} [[File:Oji jinja - Torii.jpg|thumb|left|''myōjin torii'' at the main entrance]] [[File:Oji jinja - Chōzuya.jpg|thumb|right|''Chōzuya'' at Ōji shrine]] [[File:Oji jinja - Honden 3.jpg|thumb|right|Interior of the ''Honden'']] [[File:Oji jinja - Seki Jinja 1.jpg|thumb|left|''Seki Jinja'' subsidiary shrine]] [[File:Oji jinja - Ginkgo tree 3.jpg|thumb|right|The giant ''ginkgo'' tree]] === Torii and Chōzuya === The shrine entrance is marked by a big concrete {{nihongo|''torii''|鳥居||literally bird abode}} in the [[Torii#Myōjin family|''myōjin'']] style, very common in [[Shinto architecture]], characterized by curved upper lintels. A short {{nihongo|''sandō''|参道||visiting path}} leads from the torii to the honden. On its right side stand a {{nihongo|''chōzuya''|手水舎|}}, the traditional water ablution pavilion used by worshippers to purify themselves before approaching the shrine. There is a back access to the shrine through a long stairway from the Otonashi Shinsui Park. This is where the giant ginkgo tree stands. === Honden === The main building of Ōji shrine is a {{nihongo|''[[honden]]''| 本殿|main hall}} in the ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]'' style, a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, the ''[[heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'', or offertory hall, and the ''honden'', are all interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on December 21, 2017 The [[kami]] worshipped here are [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] and [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]], considered to be "especially benevolent regarding marriage ceremonies, marital harmony, and restoring luck".{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} === Seki Shrine === At the left of the ''sandō'' stands a subsidiary shrine called ''Seki shrine''. This small shrine has been described as "rare even within Japan since it is dedicated to an ancestral deity in charge of hair."{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} === Ginkgo tree === The ''ginkgo biloba'' or ginkgo tree stands on a hill of the left bank of the Otonashi river, just beside the shrine precincts, at the right side of the sandō. It has a height of 24.2 m and a trunk circumference of 5.2 m. It is suspected to have been planted during the construction of the shrine, which would make it more than 600 years old.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} Unlike most of the Ōji shrine, the ginkgo tree survived the [[bombing of Tokyo]] during [[World War II]], although the top part of the trunk was damaged.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} One of the main attractions of the shrine, it was designated a [[List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|Natural Monument]] in 1939.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} Another giant ginkgo tree of similar characteristics in Tokyo is located in the grounds of [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]]. == Annual Events == The annual festival of Ōji Shrine is held the first Sunday of August.{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} It features a [[mikoshi]] parade, and the performance of the ''Ōji Jinja Dengakumai'' dance, one of Japan's three great [[dengaku]] dances.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == Access == There is no admission fee for visitors to enter the shrine precincts, and there is parking space available.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The entrances is at a 3-minute walk from the north exit of [[Ōji Station (Tokyo)|Ōji Station]] on the [[Keihin-Tōhoku Line]], or from exit 3 of the same station on the [[Tokyo Metro Namboku Line]], at a 5-minute walk from [[Ōji-ekimae Station]] and a 7-minute walk from [[Asukayama Station]] on the [[Toden Arakawa Line]].{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == References == {{reflist|2}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Oji jinja (Tokyo)|Oji Shrine}} *[http://ojijinja.tokyo.jp Official website] (in Japanese) {{Kita, Tokyo}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ōji Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]] [[Category:pages from wikipedia]][[en:Ōji Shrine]][[Category:pages from wikipedia]] {{Kumano Faith}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in the Kita-ku ward of Tokyo, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōji Shrine
王子神社 | image = Oji jinja - Honden.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'' of Oji shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.7533|139.7359|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]] | founded_by = | established = 1320s | date_destroyed = | location = 1 Chome-1-12 Ōjihonchō, [[Kita, Tokyo|Kita-ku]], [[Tokyo]] [[〒]] 114-0022 | website = {{URL|http://ojijinja.tokyo.jp}} | architecture_style = [[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ōji Shrine'''|王子神社|Ōji-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the [[Kita, Tokyo|Kita ward]] of [[Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. Established during the [[Kamakura period]], most likely around 1321–1324, the shrine gives the name of "Ōji" to this area of the city. Most of the original buildings in the shrine precincts were destroyed during [[World War II]], and were rebuilt from the late 50s to 1982. It is notable for its giant [[Ginkgo biloba|ginkgo tree]],{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} suspected to be 600 years old and designated a [[List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|Natural Monument]] in 1939, and for its annual festival (held in August), which includes a [[mikoshi]] parade and a performance of [[dengaku]] dance.{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} It is one of the {{Nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}},{{cite web |url=http://tencoo.fc2web.com/jinja/xzyussha.htm |title=Tokyo Ten Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher= |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} of which it has been designated as the "northern protector shrine".{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == History == The exact date in which the Ōji Shrine was founded is not known, but according to a stone with inscriptions (designated a [[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)|Tangible Cultural Property]] of Tokyo) still standing beside the ginkgo tree, near the shrine precincts, Toyoshima contributed to found the shrine between 1321 and 1324.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The giant ginkgo tree is suspected to have been planted at around this time. [[Ōta Dōkan]] (1432-1486), the [[samurai]] and [[Buddhist monk]] who designed and built the [[Edo Castle]] (now the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]]), is said to have taken shelter from a storm under a big [[castanopsis]] tree in the shrine precincts which was destroyed, probably during [[World War II]].{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The area of the [[Kita, Tokyo|Kita-ku]] [[Special wards of Tokyo|ward]] surrounding it receives the name of "Ōji" from the shrine.{{cite web |url=http://dennisamith.com/2017/11/15/the-one-about-oji-jinja-shrine-in-kita/ |title=The One about Oji Jinja Shrine in Kita |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Dennis A. Amith |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == Architecture == Most of the original buildings of Ōji Shrine were destroyed in 1945 during [[World War II]], and were rebuilt in 1959-1964[[:ja:王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社 (東京都北区)]]{{Circular reference|date=August 2023}}{{Circular reference|date=November 2020}} and in 1982.{{cite web |url=http://dennisamith.com/2017/11/15/the-one-about-oji-jinja-shrine-in-kita/ |title=The One about Oji Jinja Shrine in Kita |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Dennis A. Amith |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} [[File:Oji jinja - Torii.jpg|thumb|left|''myōjin torii'' at the main entrance]] [[File:Oji jinja - Chōzuya.jpg|thumb|right|''Chōzuya'' at Ōji shrine]] [[File:Oji jinja - Honden 3.jpg|thumb|right|Interior of the ''Honden'']] [[File:Oji jinja - Seki Jinja 1.jpg|thumb|left|''Seki Jinja'' subsidiary shrine]] [[File:Oji jinja - Ginkgo tree 3.jpg|thumb|right|The giant ''ginkgo'' tree]] === Torii and Chōzuya === The shrine entrance is marked by a big concrete {{nihongo|''torii''|鳥居||literally bird abode}} in the [[Torii#Myōjin family|''myōjin'']] style, very common in [[Shinto architecture]], characterized by curved upper lintels. On its right side stand a {{nihongo|''chōzuya''|手水舎|}}, the traditional water ablution pavilion used by worshippers to purify themselves before approaching the shrine. There is a back access to the shrine through a long stairway from the Otonashi Shinsui Park. It has a height of 24.2 m and a trunk circumference of 5.2 m. It is suspected to have been planted during the construction of the shrine, which would make it more than 600 years old.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} One of the main attractions of the shrine, it was designated a [[List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|Natural Ōji Shrine is held the first Sunday of August.{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} It features a [[mikoshi]] parade, and the performance of the ''Ōji Jinja Dengakumai'' dance, one of Japan's three great [[dengaku]] dances.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == Access == There is no admission fee for visitors to enter the shrine precincts, and there is parking space available.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The entrances is at On its right side stand a {{nihongo|''chōzuya''|手水舎|}}, the traditional water ablution pavilion used by worshippers to purify themselves before approaching the shrine. There is a back access to the shrine through a long stairway from the Otonashi Shinsui Park. This is where the giant ginkgo tree stands. === Honden === The main building of Ōji shrine is a {{nihongo|''[[honden]]''| 本殿|main hall}} in the ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]'' style, a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, the ''[[heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'', or offertory hall, and the ''honden'', are all interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on December 21, 2017 The [[kami]] worshipped here are [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] and [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]], considered to be "especially benevolent regarding marriage ceremonies, marital harmony, and restoring luck".{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} === Seki Shrine === At the left of the ''sandō'' stands a subsidiary shrine called ''Seki shrine''. This small shrine has been described as "rare even within Japan since it is dedicated to an ancestral deity in charge of hair."{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} === Ginkgo tree === The ''ginkgo biloba'' or ginkgo tree stands on a hill of the left bank of the Otonashi river, just beside the shrine precincts, at the right side of the sandō. It has a height of 24.2 m and a trunk circumference of 5.2 m. It is suspected to have been planted during the construction of the shrine, which would make it more than 600 years old.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} Unlike most of the Ōji shrine, the ginkgo tree survived the [[bombing of Tokyo]] during [[World War II]], although the top part of the trunk was damaged.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} One of the main attractions of the shrine, it was designated a [[List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|Natural Monument]] in 1939.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} Another giant ginkgo tree of similar characteristics in Tokyo is located in the grounds of [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]]. == Annual Events == The annual festival of Ōji Shrine is held the first Sunday of August.{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} It features a [[mikoshi]] parade, and the performance of the ''Ōji Jinja Dengakumai'' dance, one of Japan's three great [[dengaku]] dances.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == Access == There is no admission fee for visitors to enter the shrine precincts, and there is parking space available.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The entrances is at a 3-minute walk from the north exit of [[Ōji Station (Tokyo)|Ōji Station]] on the [[Keihin-Tōhoku Line]], or from exit 3 of the same station on the [[Tokyo Metro Namboku Line]], at a 5-minute walk from [[Ōji-ekimae Station]] and a 7-minute walk from [[Asukayama Station]] on the [[Toden Arakawa Line]].{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == References == {{reflist|2}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Oji jinja (Tokyo)|Oji Shrine}} *[http://ojijinja.tokyo.jp Official website] (in Japanese) {{Kita, Tokyo}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ōji Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia9]] [[en:Ōji Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia9]] {{Kumano Faith}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in the Kita-ku ward of Tokyo, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōji Shrine
王子神社 | image = Oji jinja - Honden.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'' of Oji shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.7533|139.7359|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]] | founded_by = | established = 1320s | date_destroyed = | location = 1 Chome-1-12 Ōjihonchō, [[Kita, Tokyo|Kita-ku]], [[Tokyo]] [[〒]] 114-0022 | website = {{URL|http://ojijinja.tokyo.jp}} | architecture_style = [[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ōji Shrine'''|王子神社|Ōji-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the [[Kita, Tokyo|Kita ward]] of [[Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. Established during the [[Kamakura period]], most likely around 1321–1324, the shrine gives the name of "Ōji" to this area of the city. Most of the original buildings in the shrine precincts were destroyed during [[World War II]], and were rebuilt from the late 50s to 1982. It is notable for its giant [[Ginkgo biloba|ginkgo tree]],{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} suspected to be 600 years old and designated a [[List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|Natural Monument]] in 1939, and for its annual festival (held in August), which includes a [[mikoshi]] parade and a performance of [[dengaku]] dance.{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} It is one of the {{Nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}},{{cite web |url=http://tencoo.fc2web.com/jinja/xzyussha.htm |title=Tokyo Ten Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher= |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} of which it has been designated as the "northern protector shrine".{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == History == The exact date in which the Ōji Shrine was founded is not known, but according to a stone with inscriptions (designated a [[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)|Tangible Cultural Property]] of Tokyo) still standing beside the ginkgo tree, near the shrine precincts, Toyoshima contributed to found the shrine between 1321 and 1324.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The giant ginkgo tree is suspected to have been planted at around this time. [[Ōta Dōkan]] (1432-1486), the [[samurai]] and [[Buddhist monk]] who designed and built the [[Edo Castle]] (now the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]]), is said to have taken shelter from a storm under a big [[castanopsis]] tree in the shrine precincts which was destroyed, probably during [[World War II]].{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The area of the [[Kita, Tokyo|Kita-ku]] [[Special wards of Tokyo|ward]] surrounding it receives the name of "Ōji" from the shrine.{{cite web |url=http://dennisamith.com/2017/11/15/the-one-about-oji-jinja-shrine-in-kita/ |title=The One about Oji Jinja Shrine in Kita |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Dennis A. Amith |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == Architecture == Most of the original buildings of Ōji Shrine were destroyed in 1945 during [[World War II]], and were rebuilt in 1959-1964[[:ja:王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社 (東京都北区)]]{{Circular reference|date=August 2023}}{{Circular reference|date=November 2020}} and in 1982.{{cite web |url=http://dennisamith.com/2017/11/15/the-one-about-oji-jinja-shrine-in-kita/ |title=The One about Oji Jinja Shrine in Kita |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Dennis A. Amith |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} [[File:Oji jinja - Torii.jpg|thumb|left|''myōjin torii'' at the main entrance]] [[File:Oji jinja - Chōzuya.jpg|thumb|right|''Chōzuya'' at Ōji shrine]] [[File:Oji jinja - Honden 3.jpg|thumb|right|Interior of the ''Honden'']] [[File:Oji jinja - Seki Jinja 1.jpg|thumb|left|''Seki Jinja'' subsidiary shrine]] [[File:Oji jinja - Ginkgo tree 3.jpg|thumb|right|The giant ''ginkgo'' tree]] === Torii and Chōzuya === The shrine entrance is marked by a big concrete {{nihongo|''torii''|鳥居||literally bird abode}} in the [[Torii#Myōjin family|''myōjin'']] style, very common in [[Shinto architecture]], characterized by curved upper lintels. A short {{nihongo|''sandō''|参道||visiting path}} leads from the torii to the honden. On its right side stand a {{nihongo|''chōzuya''|手水舎|}}, the traditional water ablution pavilion used by worshippers to purify themselves before approaching the shrine. There is a back access to the shrine through a long stairway from the Otonashi Shinsui Park. This is where the giant ginkgo tree stands. === Honden === The main building of Ōji shrine is a {{nihongo|''[[honden]]''| 本殿|main hall}} in the ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]'' style, a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, the ''[[heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'', or offertory hall, and the ''honden'', are all interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on December 21, 2017 The [[kami]] worshipped here are [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] and [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]], considered to be "especially benevolent regarding marriage ceremonies, marital harmony, and restoring luck".{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} === Seki Shrine === At the left of the ''sandō'' stands a subsidiary shrine called ''Seki shrine''. This small shrine has been described as "rare even within Japan since it is dedicated to an ancestral deity in charge of hair."{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} === Ginkgo tree === The ''ginkgo biloba'' or ginkgo tree stands on a hill of the left bank of the Otonashi river, just beside the shrine precincts, at the right side of the sandō. It has a height of 24.2 m and a trunk circumference of 5.2 m. It is suspected to have been planted during the construction of the shrine, which would make it more than 600 years old.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} Unlike most of the Ōji shrine, the ginkgo tree survived the [[bombing of Tokyo]] during [[World War II]], although the top part of the trunk was damaged.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} One of the main attractions of the shrine, it was designated a [[List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|Natural Monument]] in 1939.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} Another giant ginkgo tree of similar characteristics in Tokyo is located in the grounds of [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]]. == Annual Events == The annual festival of Ōji Shrine is held the first Sunday of August.{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} It features a [[mikoshi]] parade, and the performance of the ''Ōji Jinja Dengakumai'' dance, one of Japan's three great [[dengaku]] dances.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == Access == There is no admission fee for visitors to enter the shrine precincts, and there is parking space available.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The entrances is at a 3-minute walk from the north exit of [[Ōji Station (Tokyo)|Ōji Station]] on the [[Keihin-Tōhoku Line]], or from exit 3 of the same station on the [[Tokyo Metro Namboku Line]], at a 5-minute walk from [[Ōji-ekimae Station]] and a 7-minute walk from [[Asukayama Station]] on the [[Toden Arakawa Line]].{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == References == {{reflist|2}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Oji jinja (Tokyo)|Oji Shrine}} *[http://ojijinja.tokyo.jp Official website] (in Japanese) {{Kita, Tokyo}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ōji Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia9]] [[en:Ōji Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia9]] [[Category:Pages with bad category]] {{Kumano Faith}}

Monument]] in 1939.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} Another giant ginkgo tree of similar characteristics in Tokyo is located in the grounds of [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]]. == Annual Events == The annual festival of Ōji Shrine is held the first Sunday of August.{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} It features a [[mikoshi]] parade, and the performance of the ''Ōji Jinja Dengakumai'' dance, one of Japan's three great [[dengaku]] dances.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == Access == There is no admission fee for visitors to enter the shrine precincts, and there is parking space available.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The entrances is at a 3-minute walk from the north exit of [[Ōji Station (Tokyo)|Ōji Station]] on the [[Keihin-Tōhoku Line]], or from exit 3 of the same station on the [[Tokyo Metro Namboku Line]], at a 5-minute walk from [[Ōji-ekimae Station]] and a 7-minute walk from [[Asukayama Station]] on the [[Toden Arakawa Line]].{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == References == {{reflist|2}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Oji jinja (Tokyo)|Oji Shrine}} Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia9]] [[en:Ōji Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia9]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in the Kita-ku ward of Tokyo, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōji Shrine
王子神社 | image = Oji jinja - Honden.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'' of Oji shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.7533|139.7359|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]] | founded_by = | established = 1320s | date_destroyed = | location = 1 Chome-1-12 Ōjihonchō, [[Kita, Tokyo|Kita-ku]], [[Tokyo]] [[〒]] 114-0022 | website = {{URL|http://ojijinja.tokyo.jp}} | architecture_style = [[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ōji Shrine'''|王子神社|Ōji-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the [[Kita, Tokyo|Kita ward]] of [[Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. Established during the [[Kamakura period]], most likely around 1321–1324, the shrine gives the name of "Ōji" to this area of the city. Most of the original buildings in the shrine precincts were destroyed during [[World War II]], and were rebuilt from the late 50s to 1982. It is notable for its giant [[Ginkgo biloba|ginkgo tree]],{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} suspected to be 600 years old and designated a [[List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|Natural Monument]] in 1939, and for its annual festival (held in August), which includes a [[mikoshi]] parade and a performance of [[dengaku]] dance.{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} It is one of the {{Nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}},{{cite web |url=http://tencoo.fc2web.com/jinja/xzyussha.htm |title=Tokyo Ten Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher= |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} of which it has been designated as the "northern protector shrine".{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == History == The exact date in which the Ōji Shrine was founded is not known, but according to a stone with inscriptions (designated a [[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)|Tangible Cultural Property]] of Tokyo) still standing beside the ginkgo tree, near the shrine precincts, Toyoshima contributed to found the shrine between 1321 and 1324.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The giant ginkgo tree is suspected to have been planted at around this time. [[Ōta Dōkan]] (1432-1486), the [[samurai]] and [[Buddhist monk]] who designed and built the [[Edo Castle]] (now the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]]), is said to have taken shelter from a storm under a big [[castanopsis]] tree in the shrine precincts which was destroyed, probably during [[World War II]].{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The area of the [[Kita, Tokyo|Kita-ku]] [[Special wards of Tokyo|ward]] surrounding it receives the name of "Ōji" from the shrine.{{cite web |url=http://dennisamith.com/2017/11/15/the-one-about-oji-jinja-shrine-in-kita/ |title=The One about Oji Jinja Shrine in Kita |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Dennis A. Amith |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == Architecture == Most of the original buildings of Ōji Shrine were destroyed in 1945 during [[World War II]], and were rebuilt in 1959-1964[[:ja:王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社 (東京都北区)]]{{Circular reference|date=August 2023}}{{Circular reference|date=November 2020}} and in 1982.{{cite web |url=http://dennisamith.com/2017/11/15/the-one-about-oji-jinja-shrine-in-kita/ |title=The One about Oji Jinja Shrine in Kita |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Dennis A. Amith |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} [[File:Oji jinja - Torii.jpg|thumb|left|''myōjin torii'' at the main entrance]] [[File:Oji jinja - Chōzuya.jpg|thumb|right|''Chōzuya'' at Ōji shrine]] [[File:Oji jinja - Honden 3.jpg|thumb|right|Interior of the ''Honden'']] [[File:Oji jinja - Seki Jinja 1.jpg|thumb|left|''Seki Jinja'' subsidiary shrine]] [[File:Oji jinja - Ginkgo tree 3.jpg|thumb|right|The giant ''ginkgo'' tree]] === Torii and Chōzuya === The shrine entrance is marked by a big concrete {{nihongo|''torii''|鳥居||literally bird abode}} in the [[Torii#Myōjin family|''myōjin'']] style, very common in [[Shinto architecture]], characterized by curved upper lintels. A short {{nihongo|''sandō''|参道||visiting path}} leads from the torii to the honden. On its right side stand a {{nihongo|''chōzuya''|手水舎|}}, the traditional water ablution pavilion used by worshippers to purify themselves before approaching the shrine. There is a back access to the shrine through a long stairway from the Otonashi Shinsui Park. This is where the giant ginkgo tree stands. === Honden === The main building of Ōji shrine is a {{nihongo|''[[honden]]''| 本殿|main hall}} in the ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]'' style, a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, the ''[[heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'', or offertory hall, and the ''honden'', are all interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on December 21, 2017 The [[kami]] worshipped here are [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] and [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]], considered to be "especially benevolent regarding marriage ceremonies, marital harmony, and restoring luck".{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} === Seki Shrine === At the left of the ''sandō'' stands a subsidiary shrine called ''Seki shrine''. This small shrine has been described as "rare even within Japan since it is dedicated to an ancestral deity in charge of hair."{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} === Ginkgo tree === The ''ginkgo biloba'' or ginkgo tree stands on a hill of the left bank of the Otonashi river, just beside the shrine precincts, at the right side of the sandō. It has a height of 24.2 m and a trunk circumference of 5.2 m. It is suspected to have been planted during the construction of the shrine, which would make it more than 600 years old.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} Unlike most of the Ōji shrine, the ginkgo tree survived the [[bombing of Tokyo]] during [[World War II]], although the top part of the trunk was damaged.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} One of the main attractions of the shrine, it was designated a [[List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|Natural Monument]] in 1939.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} Another giant ginkgo tree of similar characteristics in Tokyo is located in the grounds of [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]]. == Annual Events == The annual festival of Ōji Shrine is held the first Sunday of August.{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} It features a [[mikoshi]] parade, and the performance of the ''Ōji Jinja Dengakumai'' dance, one of Japan's three great [[dengaku]] dances.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == Access == There is no admission fee for visitors to enter the shrine precincts, and there is parking space available.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The entrances is at a 3-minute walk from the north exit of [[Ōji Station (Tokyo)|Ōji Station]] on the [[Keihin-Tōhoku Line]], or from exit 3 of the same station on the [[Tokyo Metro Namboku Line]], at a 5-minute walk from [[Ōji-ekimae Station]] and a 7-minute walk from [[Asukayama Station]] on the [[Toden Arakawa Line]].{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == References == {{reflist|2}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Oji jinja (Tokyo)|Oji Shrine}} *[http://ojijinja.tokyo.jp Official website] (in Japanese) {{Kita, Tokyo}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ōji Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia9]] [[en:Ōji Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia9]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia0]] {{Kumano Faith}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in the Kita-ku ward of Tokyo, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōji Shrine
王子神社 | image = Oji jinja - Honden.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'' of Oji shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.7533|139.7359|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]] | founded_by = | established = 1320s | date_destroyed = | location = 1 Chome-1-12 Ōjihonchō, [[Kita, Tokyo|Kita-ku]], [[Tokyo]] [[〒]] 114-0022 | website = {{URL|http://ojijinja.tokyo.jp}} | architecture_style = [[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ōji Shrine'''|王子神社|Ōji-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the [[Kita, Tokyo|Kita ward]] of [[Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. Established during the [[Kamakura period]], most likely around 1321–1324, the shrine gives the name of "Ōji" to this area of the city. Most of the original buildings in the shrine precincts were destroyed during [[World War II]], and were rebuilt from the late 50s to 1982. It is notable for its giant [[Ginkgo biloba|ginkgo tree]],{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} suspected to be 600 years old and designated a [[List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|Natural Monument]] in 1939, and for its annual festival (held in August), which includes a [[mikoshi]] parade and a performance of [[dengaku]] dance.{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} It is one of the {{Nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}},{{cite web |url=http://tencoo.fc2web.com/jinja/xzyussha.htm |title=Tokyo Ten Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher= |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} of which it has been designated as the "northern protector shrine".{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == History == The exact date in which the Ōji Shrine was founded is not known, but according to a stone with inscriptions (designated a [[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)|Tangible Cultural Property]] of Tokyo) still standing beside the ginkgo tree, near the shrine precincts, Toyoshima contributed to found the shrine between 1321 and 1324.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The giant ginkgo tree is suspected to have been planted at around this time. [[Ōta Dōkan]] (1432-1486), the [[samurai]] and [[Buddhist monk]] who designed and built the [[Edo Castle]] (now the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]]), is said to have taken shelter from a storm under a big [[castanopsis]] tree in the shrine precincts which was destroyed, probably during [[World War II]].{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The area of the [[Kita, Tokyo|Kita-ku]] [[Special wards of Tokyo|ward]] surrounding it receives the name of "Ōji" from the shrine.{{cite web |url=http://dennisamith.com/2017/11/15/the-one-about-oji-jinja-shrine-in-kita/ |title=The One about Oji Jinja Shrine in Kita |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Dennis A. Amith |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == Architecture == Most of the original buildings of Ōji Shrine were destroyed in 1945 during [[World War II]], and were rebuilt in 1959-1964[[:ja:王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社 (東京都北区)]]{{Circular reference|date=August 2023}}{{Circular reference|date=November 2020}} and in 1982.{{cite web |url=http://dennisamith.com/2017/11/15/the-one-about-oji-jinja-shrine-in-kita/ |title=The One about Oji Jinja Shrine in Kita |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Dennis A. Amith |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} [[File:Oji jinja - Torii.jpg|thumb|left|''myōjin torii'' at the main entrance]] [[File:Oji jinja - Chōzuya.jpg|thumb|right|''Chōzuya'' at Ōji shrine]] [[File:Oji jinja - Honden 3.jpg|thumb|right|Interior of the ''Honden'']] [[File:Oji jinja - Seki Jinja 1.jpg|thumb|left|''Seki Jinja'' subsidiary shrine]] [[File:Oji jinja - Ginkgo tree 3.jpg|thumb|right|The giant ''ginkgo'' tree]] === Torii and Chōzuya === The shrine entrance is marked by a big concrete {{nihongo|''torii''|鳥居||literally bird abode}} in the [[Torii#Myōjin family|''myōjin'']] style, very common in [[Shinto architecture]], characterized by curved upper lintels. A short {{nihongo|''sandō''|参道||visiting path}} leads from the torii to the honden. On its right side stand a {{nihongo|''chōzuya''|手水舎|}}, the traditional water ablution pavilion used by worshippers to purify themselves before approaching the shrine. There is a back access to the shrine through a long stairway from the Otonashi Shinsui Park. This is where the giant ginkgo tree stands. === Honden === The main building of Ōji shrine is a {{nihongo|''[[honden]]''| 本殿|main hall}} in the ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]'' style, a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, the ''[[heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'', or offertory hall, and the ''honden'', are all interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on December 21, 2017 The [[kami]] worshipped here are [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] and [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]], considered to be "especially benevolent regarding marriage ceremonies, marital harmony, and restoring luck".{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} === Seki Shrine === At the left of the ''sandō'' stands a subsidiary shrine called ''Seki shrine''. This small shrine has been described as "rare even within Japan since it is dedicated to an ancestral deity in charge of hair."{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} === Ginkgo tree === The ''ginkgo biloba'' or ginkgo tree stands on a hill of the left bank of the Otonashi river, just beside the shrine precincts, at the right side of the sandō. It has a height of 24.2 m and a trunk circumference of 5.2 m. It is suspected to have been planted during the construction of the shrine, which would make it more than 600 years old.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} Unlike most of the Ōji shrine, the ginkgo tree survived the [[bombing of Tokyo]] during [[World War II]], although the top part of the trunk was damaged.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} One of the main attractions of the shrine, it was designated a [[List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|Natural Monument]] in 1939.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} Another giant ginkgo tree of similar characteristics in Tokyo is located in the grounds of [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]]. == Annual Events == The annual festival of Ōji Shrine is held the first Sunday of August.{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} It features a [[mikoshi]] parade, and the performance of the ''Ōji Jinja Dengakumai'' dance, one of Japan's three great [[dengaku]] dances.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == Access == There is no admission fee for visitors to enter the shrine precincts, and there is parking space available.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The entrances is at a 3-minute walk from the north exit of [[Ōji Station (Tokyo)|Ōji Station]] on the [[Keihin-Tōhoku Line]], or from exit 3 of the same station on the [[Tokyo Metro Namboku Line]], at a 5-minute walk from [[Ōji-ekimae Station]] and a 7-minute walk from [[Asukayama Station]] on the [[Toden Arakawa Line]].{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == References == {{reflist|2}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Oji jinja (Tokyo)|Oji Shrine}} *[http://ojijinja.tokyo.jp Official website] (in Japanese) {{Kita, Tokyo}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ōji Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia9]] [[en:Ōji Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia9]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia0]] [[en:Ōji Shrine]]{{Kumano Faith}}

|title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == History == The exact date in which the Ōji Shrine was founded is not known, but according to a stone with inscriptions (designated a [[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)|Tangible Cultural Property]] of Tokyo) still standing beside the ginkgo tree, near the shrine precincts, Toyoshima contributed to found the shrine between 1321 and 1324.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The giant ginkgo tree is suspected to have been planted at around this time. [[Ōta Dōkan]] (1432-1486), the [[samurai]] and [[Buddhist monk]] who designed and built the [[Edo Castle]] (now the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]]), is said to have taken shelter from a storm under a big [[castanopsis]] tree in the shrine precincts which was destroyed, probably during [[World War II]].{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The area of the [[Kita, Tokyo|Kita-ku]] [[Special wards of Tokyo|ward]] surrounding it receives the name of "Ōji" from the shrine.{{cite web |url=http://dennisamith.com/2017/11/15/the-one-about-oji-jinja-shrine-in-kita/ |title=The One about Oji Jinja Shrine in Kita |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Dennis A. Amith |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == Architecture == Most of the original buildings of Ōji Shrine were destroyed in 1945 during [[World War II]], and were rebuilt in 1959-1964[[:ja:王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社 (東京都北区)]]{{Circular reference|date=August 2023}}{{Circular reference|date=November 2020}} and in 1982.{{cite web |url=http://dennisamith.com/2017/11/15/the-one-about-oji-jinja-shrine-in-kita/ |title=The One about Oji Jinja Shrine in Kita |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Dennis A. Amith |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} [[File:Oji jinja - Torii.jpg|thumb|left|''myōjin torii'' at the main entrance]] [[File:Oji jinja - Chōzuya.jpg|thumb|right|''Chōzuya'' at Ōji shrine]] [[File:Oji jinja - Honden 3.jpg|thumb|right|Interior of the ''Honden'']] [[File:Oji jinja - Seki Jinja 1.jpg|thumb|left|''Seki Jinja'' subsidiary shrine]] [[File:Oji jinja - Ginkgo tree 3.jpg|thumb|right|The giant ''ginkgo'' tree]] === Torii and Chōzuya === The shrine entrance is marked by a big concrete {{nihongo|''torii''|鳥居||literally bird abode}} in the [[Torii#Myōjin family|''myōjin'']] style, very common in [[Shinto architecture]], characterized by curved upper lintels. A short {{nihongo|''sandō''|参道||visiting path}} leads from the torii to the honden. On its right side stand a {{nihongo|''chōzuya''|手水舎|}}, the traditional water ablution pavilion used by worshippers to purify themselves before approaching the shrine. There is a back access to the shrine through a long stairway from the Otonashi Shinsui Park. This is where the giant ginkgo tree stands. === Honden === The main building of Ōji shrine is a {{nihongo|''[[honden]]''| 本殿|main hall}} in the ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]'' style, a complex [[Shinto shrine]] structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, the ''[[heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'', or offertory hall, and the ''honden'', are all interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on December 21, 2017 The [[kami]] worshipped here are [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]] and [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]], considered to be "especially benevolent regarding marriage ceremonies, marital harmony, and restoring luck".{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} === Seki Shrine === At the left of the ''sandō'' stands a subsidiary shrine called ''Seki shrine''. This small shrine has been described as "rare even within Japan since it is dedicated to an ancestral deity in charge of hair."{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} === Ginkgo tree === The ''ginkgo biloba'' or ginkgo tree stands on a hill of the left bank of the Otonashi river, just beside the shrine precincts, at the right side of the sandō. It has a height of 24.2 m and a trunk circumference of 5.2 m. It is suspected to have been planted during the construction of the shrine, which would make it more than 600 years old.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} Unlike most of the Ōji shrine, the ginkgo tree survived the [[bombing of Tokyo]] during [[World War II]], although the top part of the trunk was damaged.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} One of the main attractions of the shrine, it was designated a [[List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|Natural Monument]] in 1939.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} Another giant ginkgo tree of similar characteristics in Tokyo is located in the grounds of [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]]. == Annual Events == The annual festival of Ōji Shrine is held the first Sunday of August.{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} It features a [[mikoshi]] parade, and the performance of the ''Ōji Jinja Dengakumai'' dance, one of Japan's three great [[dengaku]] dances.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == Access == There is no admission fee for visitors to enter the shrine precincts, and there is parking space available.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The entrances is at a 3-minute walk from the north exit of [[Ōji Station (Tokyo)|Ōji Station]] on the [[Keihin-Tōhoku Line]], or from exit 3 of the same station on the [[Tokyo Metro Namboku Line]], at a 5-minute walk from [[Ōji-ekimae Station]] and a 7-minute walk from [[Asukayama Station]] on the [[Toden Arakawa Line]].{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == References == {{reflist|2}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Oji jinja (Tokyo)|Oji Shrine}} *[http://ojijinja.tokyo.jp Official website] (in Japanese) {{Kita, Tokyo}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ōji Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia9]] [[en:Ōji Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia9]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia0]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia25]] [[en:Ōji Shrine]]{{Kumano Faith}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in the Kita-ku ward of Tokyo, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōji Shrine
王子神社 | image = Oji jinja - Honden.jpg | caption = The ''[[honden]]'' of Oji shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.7533|139.7359|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]] | founded_by = | established = 1320s | date_destroyed = | location = 1 Chome-1-12 Ōjihonchō, [[Kita, Tokyo|Kita-ku]], [[Tokyo]] [[〒]] 114-0022 | website = {{URL|http://ojijinja.tokyo.jp}} | architecture_style = [[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ōji Shrine'''|王子神社|Ōji-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the [[Kita, Tokyo|Kita ward]] of [[Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. Established during the [[Kamakura period]], most likely around 1321–1324, the shrine gives the name of "Ōji" to this area of the city. Most of the original buildings in the shrine precincts were destroyed during [[World War II]], and were rebuilt from the late 50s to 1982. It is notable for its giant [[Ginkgo biloba|ginkgo tree]],{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} suspected to be 600 years old and designated a [[List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|Natural Monument]] in 1939, and for its annual festival (held in August), which includes a [[mikoshi]] parade and a performance of [[dengaku]] dance.{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} It is one of the {{Nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}},{{cite web |url=http://tencoo.fc2web.com/jinja/xzyussha.htm |title=Tokyo Ten Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher= |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} of which it has been designated as the "northern protector shrine".{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == History == The exact date in which the Ōji Shrine was founded is not precincts, Toyoshima contributed to found the shrine between 1321 and 1324.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The giant ginkgo tree is suspected to have been planted at around this time. [[Ōta Dōkan]] (1432-1486), the [[samurai]] and [[Buddhist monk]] who designed and built the [[Edo Castle]] (now the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]]), is said to have taken shelter from a storm under a big [[castanopsis]] tree in the shrine precincts which was destroyed, probably during [[World War II]].{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The area of the [[Kita, Tokyo|Kita-ku]] [[Special wards of Tokyo|ward]] surrounding it receives the name of "Ōji" from the shrine.{{cite web |url=http://dennisamith.com/2017/11/15/the-one-about-oji-jinja-shrine-in-kita/ |title=The One about Oji Jinja Shrine in Kita |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Dennis A. Amith |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == Architecture == Most of the original buildings of Ōji Shrine were destroyed in 1945 during [[World War II]], and were rebuilt in 1959-1964[[:ja:王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社 (東京都北区)]]{{Circular reference|date=August 2023}}{{Circular reference|date=November 2020}} and in 1982.{{cite web |url=http://dennisamith.com/2017/11/15/the-one-about-oji-jinja-shrine-in-kita/ |title=The One about Oji Jinja Shrine in Kita |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Dennis A. On its right side stand a {{nihongo|''chōzuya''|手水舎|}}, the traditional water ablution pavilion used by worshippers to purify themselves before approaching the shrine. There is a back access to the shrine through a long stairway from the Otonashi Shinsui Park. This small shrine has been described as "rare even within Japan since it is dedicated to an ancestral deity in charge of hair."{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} === Ginkgo tree === The ''ginkgo biloba'' or ginkgo tree stands on a hill of the left bank of the Otonashi river, just beside the shrine precincts, at the right side of the sandō. It has a height of 24.2 m and a trunk circumference of 5.2 m. It is suspected to have been planted during the construction of the shrine, which would make it more than 600 years old.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} Unlike most of the Ōji shrine, the ginkgo tree survived the [[bombing of Tokyo]] during [[World War II]], although the top part of the trunk was damaged.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} One of the main attractions of the shrine, it was designated a [[List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|Natural Monument]] in 1939.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} Another giant ginkgo tree of similar characteristics in Tokyo is located in the grounds of [[Shiba Tōshō-gū]]. == Annual Events == The annual festival of Ōji Shrine is held the first Sunday of August.{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} It features a [[mikoshi]] parade, and the performance of the ''Ōji Jinja Dengakumai'' dance, one of Japan's three great [[dengaku]] dances.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == Access == There is no admission fee for visitors to enter the shrine precincts, and there is parking space available.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The entrances is at a 3-minute walk from the north exit of [[Ōji Station (Tokyo)|Ōji Station]] on the [[Keihin-Tōhoku Line]], or from exit 3 of the same station on the [[Tokyo Metro Namboku Line]], at a 5-minute walk from [[Ōji-ekimae Station]] and a 7-minute walk from [[Asukayama Station]] on the [[Toden Arakawa Line]].{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine
