[[co:commons:Category:Oji jinja (Tokyo)]][[en:Ōji Shrine]][[ja:王子神社 (東京都北区)]][[pl:Ōji-jinja]][[zh:王子神社 (東京都北區)]] [[co:commons:Category:Oji jinja (Tokyo)]][[en:Ōji Shrine]][[ja:王子神社 (東京都北区)]][[pl:Ōji-jinja]][[zh:王子神社 (東京都北區)]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Oji jinja (Tokyo)]][[en:Ōji Shrine]][[ja:王子神社 (東京都北区)]][[pl:Ōji-jinja]][[zh:王子神社 (東京都北區)]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in the Kita-ku ward of Tokyo, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōji Shrine
王子神社 | image = File:Oji Shrine 01.JPG | caption = The ''[[honden]]'' of Oji shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.75327778|N|139.73591667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}}; {{ill|Izanagi and Izanami|WD=Q12259961|1=Izanagi and Izanami}} | founded_by = | established = 1320s | date_destroyed = | location = 1 Chome-1-12 Ōjihonchō, {{ill|Kita, Tokyo|en|Kita, Tokyo|WD=Q235130|lt=Kita-ku|1=Kita, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}} [[:en:〒|〒]] 114-0022 | website = {{URL|http://ojijinja.tokyo.jp}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|WD=Q3155268|1=Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} | festival = | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Ōji-Honchō|WD=Q11572988|1=Ōji-Honchō}} | part_of= {{ill|Tokyo-jissha|WD=Q164895|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines}} }} {{nihongo|'''Ōji Shrine'''|王子神社|Ōji-jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in the {{ill|Kita, Tokyo|en|Kita, Tokyo|WD=Q235130|lt=Kita ward|1=Kita, Tokyo}} of {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Established during the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, most likely around 1321–1324, the shrine gives the name of "Ōji" to this area of the city. Most of the original buildings in the shrine precincts were destroyed during {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, and were rebuilt from the late 50s to 1982. It is notable for its giant {{ill|Ginkgo biloba|en|Ginkgo biloba|WD=Q43284|lt=ginkgo tree|1=Ginkgo biloba}},{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} suspected to be 600 years old and designated a {{ill|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|en|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|WD=Q20982902|lt=Natural Monument|1=List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments}} in 1939, and for its annual festival (held in August), which includes a [[mikoshi]] parade and a performance of {{ill|Dengaku|en|Dengaku|WD=Q3022455|lt=Dengaku|1=Dengaku}} dance.{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} It is one of the {{Nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}},{{cite web |url=http://tencoo.fc2web.com/jinja/xzyussha.htm |title=Tokyo Ten Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher= |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} of which it has been designated as the "northern protector shrine".{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == History == The exact date in which the Ōji Shrine was founded is not known, but according to a stone with inscriptions (designated a [[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)|Tangible Cultural Property]] of Tokyo) still standing beside the ginkgo tree, near the shrine precincts, Toyoshima contributed to found the shrine between 1321 and 1324.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The giant ginkgo tree is suspected to have been planted at around this time. {{ill|Ōta Dōkan|en|Ōta Dōkan|WD=Q386144|lt=Ōta Dōkan|1=Ōta Dōkan}} (1432-1486), the {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} and [[:en:Buddhist monk|Buddhist monk]] who designed and built the {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle|1=Edo Castle}} (now the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]]), is said to have taken shelter from a storm under a big {{ill|Castanopsis|en|Castanopsis|WD=Q1065400|lt=Castanopsis|1=Castanopsis}} tree in the shrine precincts which was destroyed, probably during {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The area of the {{ill|Kita, Tokyo|en|Kita, Tokyo|WD=Q235130|lt=Kita-ku|1=Kita, Tokyo}} {{ill|Special wards of Tokyo|en|Special wards of Tokyo|WD=Q308891|lt=ward|1=Special wards of Tokyo}} surrounding it receives the name of "Ōji" from the shrine.{{cite web |url=http://dennisamith.com/2017/11/15/the-one-about-oji-jinja-shrine-in-kita/ |title=The One about Oji Jinja Shrine in Kita |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Dennis A. Amith |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == Architecture == Most of the original buildings of Ōji Shrine were destroyed in 1945 during {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, and were rebuilt in 1959-1964[[:ja:王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社 (東京都北区)]]{{Circular reference|date=August 2023}}{{Circular reference|date=November 2020}} and in 1982.{{cite web |url=http://dennisamith.com/2017/11/15/the-one-about-oji-jinja-shrine-in-kita/ |title=The One about Oji Jinja Shrine in Kita |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Dennis A. Amith |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} [[File:Oji jinja - Torii.jpg|thumb|left|''myōjin torii'' at the main entrance]] [[File:Oji jinja - Chōzuya.jpg|thumb|right|''Chōzuya'' at Ōji shrine]] [[File:Oji jinja - Honden 3.jpg|thumb|right|Interior of the ''Honden'']] [[File:Oji jinja - Seki Jinja 1.jpg|thumb|left|''Seki Jinja'' subsidiary shrine]] [[File:Oji jinja - Ginkgo tree 3.jpg|thumb|right|The giant ''ginkgo'' tree]] === Torii and Chōzuya === The shrine entrance is marked by a big concrete {{nihongo|''torii''|鳥居||literally bird abode}} in the [[Torii#Myōjin family|''myōjin'']] style, very common in {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=Shinto architecture|1=Shinto architecture}}, characterized by curved upper lintels. A short {{nihongo|''sandō''|参道||visiting path}} leads from the torii to the honden. On its right side stand a {{nihongo|''chōzuya''|手水舎|}}, the traditional water ablution pavilion used by worshippers to purify themselves before approaching the shrine. There is a back access to the shrine through a long stairway from the Otonashi Shinsui Park. This is where the giant ginkgo tree stands. === Honden === The main building of Ōji shrine is a {{nihongo|''[[honden]]''| 本殿|main hall}} in the ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]'' style, a complex {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, the ''[[heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'', or offertory hall, and the ''honden'', are all interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on December 21, 2017 The [[kami]] worshipped here are {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}} and [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]], considered to be "especially benevolent regarding marriage ceremonies, marital harmony, and restoring luck".{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} === Seki Shrine === At the left of the ''sandō'' stands a subsidiary shrine called ''Seki shrine''. This small shrine has been described as "rare even within Japan since it is dedicated to an ancestral deity in charge of hair."{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} === Ginkgo tree === The ''ginkgo biloba'' or ginkgo tree stands on a hill of the left bank of the Otonashi river, just beside the shrine precincts, at the right side of the sandō. It has a height of 24.2 m and a trunk circumference of 5.2 m. It is suspected to have been planted during the construction of the shrine, which would make it more than 600 years old.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} Unlike most of the Ōji shrine, the ginkgo tree survived the {{ill|Bombing of Tokyo|en|Bombing of Tokyo|WD=Q59886|lt=Bombing of Tokyo|1=Bombing of Tokyo}} during {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, although the top part of the trunk was damaged.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} One of the main attractions of the shrine, it was designated a {{ill|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|en|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|WD=Q20982902|lt=Natural Monument|1=List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments}} in 1939.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} Another giant ginkgo tree of similar characteristics in Tokyo is located in the grounds of {{ill|Shiba Tōshō-gū|en|Shiba Tōshō-gū|WD=Q11614668|lt=Shiba Tōshō-gū|1=Shiba Tōshō-gū}}. == Annual Events == The annual festival of Ōji Shrine is held the first Sunday of August.{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} It features a [[mikoshi]] parade, and the performance of the ''Ōji Jinja Dengakumai'' dance, one of Japan's three great {{ill|Dengaku|en|Dengaku|WD=Q3022455|lt=Dengaku|1=Dengaku}} dances.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == Access == There is no admission fee for visitors to enter the shrine precincts, and there is parking space available.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The entrances is at a 3-minute walk from the north exit of {{ill|Ōji Station (Tokyo)|en|Ōji Station (Tokyo)|WD=Q801689|lt=Ōji Station|1=Ōji Station (Tokyo)}} on the [[:en:Keihin-Tōhoku Line|Keihin-Tōhoku Line]], or from exit 3 of the same station on the {{ill|Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|en|Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|WD=Q520772|lt=Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|1=Tokyo Metro Namboku Line}}, at a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Ōji-ekimae Station|en|Ōji-ekimae Station|WD=Q16564572|lt=Ōji-ekimae Station|1=Ōji-ekimae Station}} and a 7-minute walk from {{ill|Asukayama Station|en|Asukayama Station|WD=Q4811868|lt=Asukayama Station|1=Asukayama Station}} on the {{ill|Toden Arakawa Line|en|Toden Arakawa Line|WD=Q1064795|lt=Toden Arakawa Line|1=Toden Arakawa Line}}.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == References == {{reflist|2}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Oji jinja (Tokyo)|Oji Shrine}} *[http://ojijinja.tokyo.jp Official website] (in Japanese) {{Kita, Tokyo}} ‎9]] ‎9]] ‎]]{{Kumano Faith}} 王子神社 | image = File:Oji Shrine 01.JPG | caption = The ''[[honden]]'' of Oji shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.75327778|N|139.73591667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}}; {{ill|Izanagi and Izanami|WD=Q12259961|1=Izanagi and Izanami}} | founded_by = | established = 1320s | date_destroyed = | location = 1 Chome-1-12 Ōjihonchō, {{ill|Kita, Tokyo|en|Kita, Tokyo|WD=Q235130|lt=Kita-ku|1=Kita, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}} [[:en:〒|〒]] 114-0022 | website = {{URL|http://ojijinja.tokyo.jp}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|WD=Q3155268|1=Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} | festival = | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Ōji-Honchō|WD=Q11572988|1=Ōji-Honchō}} | part_of= {{ill|Tokyo-jissha|WD=Q164895|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines}} }} {{nihongo|'''Ōji Shrine'''|王子神社|Ōji-jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in the {{ill|Kita, Tokyo|en|Kita, Tokyo|WD=Q235130|lt=Kita ward|1=Kita, Tokyo}} of {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Established during the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, most likely around 1321–1324, the shrine gives the name of "Ōji" to this area of the city. Most of the original buildings in the shrine precincts were destroyed during {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, and were rebuilt from the late 50s to 1982. It is notable for its giant {{ill|Ginkgo biloba|en|Ginkgo biloba|WD=Q43284|lt=ginkgo tree|1=Ginkgo biloba}},{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} suspected to be 600 years old and designated a {{ill|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|en|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|WD=Q20982902|lt=Natural Monument|1=List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments}} in 1939, and for its annual festival (held in August), which includes a [[mikoshi]] parade and a performance of {{ill|Dengaku|en|Dengaku|WD=Q3022455|lt=Dengaku|1=Dengaku}} dance.{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} It is one of the {{Nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}},{{cite web |url=http://tencoo.fc2web.com/jinja/xzyussha.htm |title=Tokyo Ten Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher= |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} of which it has been designated as the "northern protector shrine".{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == History == The exact date in which the Ōji Shrine was founded is not known, but according to a stone with inscriptions (designated a [[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)|Tangible Cultural Property]] of Tokyo) still standing beside the ginkgo tree, near the shrine precincts, Toyoshima contributed to found the shrine between 1321 and 1324.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The giant ginkgo tree is suspected to have been planted at around this time. {{ill|Ōta Dōkan|en|Ōta Dōkan|WD=Q386144|lt=Ōta Dōkan|1=Ōta Dōkan}} (1432-1486), the {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} and [[:en:Buddhist monk|Buddhist monk]] who designed and built the {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle|1=Edo Castle}} (now the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]]), is said to have taken shelter from a storm under a big {{ill|Castanopsis|en|Castanopsis|WD=Q1065400|lt=Castanopsis|1=Castanopsis}} tree in the shrine precincts which was destroyed, probably during {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The area of the {{ill|Kita, Tokyo|en|Kita, Tokyo|WD=Q235130|lt=Kita-ku|1=Kita, Tokyo}} {{ill|Special wards of Tokyo|en|Special wards of Tokyo|WD=Q308891|lt=ward|1=Special wards of Tokyo}} surrounding it receives the name of "Ōji" from the shrine.{{cite web |url=http://dennisamith.com/2017/11/15/the-one-about-oji-jinja-shrine-in-kita/ |title=The One about Oji Jinja Shrine in Kita |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Dennis A. Amith |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == Architecture == Most of the original buildings of Ōji Shrine were destroyed in 1945 during {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, and were rebuilt in 1959-1964[[:ja:王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社 (東京都北区)]]{{Circular reference|date=August 2023}}{{Circular reference|date=November 2020}} and in 1982.{{cite web |url=http://dennisamith.com/2017/11/15/the-one-about-oji-jinja-shrine-in-kita/ |title=The One about Oji Jinja Shrine in Kita |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Dennis A. Amith |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} [[File:Oji jinja - Torii.jpg|thumb|left|''myōjin torii'' at the main entrance]] [[File:Oji jinja - Chōzuya.jpg|thumb|right|''Chōzuya'' at Ōji shrine]] [[File:Oji jinja - Honden 3.jpg|thumb|right|Interior of the ''Honden'']] [[File:Oji jinja - Seki Jinja 1.jpg|thumb|left|''Seki Jinja'' subsidiary shrine]] [[File:Oji jinja - Ginkgo tree 3.jpg|thumb|right|The giant ''ginkgo'' tree]] === Torii and Chōzuya === The shrine entrance is marked by a big concrete {{nihongo|''torii''|鳥居||literally bird abode}} in the [[Torii#Myōjin family|''myōjin'']] style, very common in {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=Shinto architecture|1=Shinto architecture}}, characterized by curved upper lintels. A short {{nihongo|''sandō''|参道||visiting path}} leads from the torii to the honden. On its right side stand a {{nihongo|''chōzuya''|手水舎|}}, the traditional water ablution pavilion used by worshippers to purify themselves before approaching the shrine. There is a back access to the shrine through a long stairway from the Otonashi Shinsui Park. This is where the giant ginkgo tree stands. === Honden === The main building of Ōji shrine is a {{nihongo|''[[honden]]''| 本殿|main hall}} in the ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]'' style, a complex {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, the ''[[heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'', or offertory hall, and the ''honden'', are all interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on December 21, 2017 The [[kami]] worshipped here are {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}} and [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]], considered to be "especially benevolent regarding marriage ceremonies, marital harmony, and restoring luck".{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} === Seki Shrine === At the left of the ''sandō'' stands a subsidiary shrine called ''Seki shrine''. This small shrine has been described as "rare even within Japan since it is dedicated to an ancestral deity in charge of hair."{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} === Ginkgo tree === The ''ginkgo biloba'' or ginkgo tree stands on a hill of the left bank of the Otonashi river, just beside the shrine precincts, at the right side of the sandō. It has a height of 24.2 m and a trunk circumference of 5.2 m. It is suspected to have been planted during the construction of the shrine, which would make it more than 600 years old.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} Unlike most of the Ōji shrine, the ginkgo tree survived the {{ill|Bombing of Tokyo|en|Bombing of Tokyo|WD=Q59886|lt=Bombing of Tokyo|1=Bombing of Tokyo}} during {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, although the top part of the trunk was damaged.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} One of the main attractions of the shrine, it was designated a {{ill|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|en|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|WD=Q20982902|lt=Natural Monument|1=List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments}} in 1939.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} Another giant ginkgo tree of similar characteristics in Tokyo is located in the grounds of {{ill|Shiba Tōshō-gū|en|Shiba Tōshō-gū|WD=Q11614668|lt=Shiba Tōshō-gū|1=Shiba Tōshō-gū}}. == Annual Events == The annual festival of Ōji Shrine is held the first Sunday of August.{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} It features a [[mikoshi]] parade, and the performance of the ''Ōji Jinja Dengakumai'' dance, one of Japan's three great {{ill|Dengaku|en|Dengaku|WD=Q3022455|lt=Dengaku|1=Dengaku}} dances.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == Access == There is no admission fee for visitors to enter the shrine precincts, and there is parking space available.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The entrances is at a 3-minute walk from the north exit of {{ill|Ōji Station (Tokyo)|en|Ōji Station (Tokyo)|WD=Q801689|lt=Ōji Station|1=Ōji Station (Tokyo)}} on the [[:en:Keihin-Tōhoku Line|Keihin-Tōhoku Line]], or from exit 3 of the same station on the {{ill|Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|en|Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|WD=Q520772|lt=Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|1=Tokyo Metro Namboku Line}}, at a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Ōji-ekimae Station|en|Ōji-ekimae Station|WD=Q16564572|lt=Ōji-ekimae Station|1=Ōji-ekimae Station}} and a 7-minute walk from {{ill|Asukayama Station|en|Asukayama Station|WD=Q4811868|lt=Asukayama Station|1=Asukayama Station}} on the {{ill|Toden Arakawa Line|en|Toden Arakawa Line|WD=Q1064795|lt=Toden Arakawa Line|1=Toden Arakawa Line}}.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == References == {{reflist|2}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Oji jinja (Tokyo)|Oji Shrine}} *[http://ojijinja.tokyo.jp Official website] (in Japanese) {{Kita, Tokyo}} ‎9]] ‎9]] ‎]]{{Kumano Faith}} revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Oji jinja (Tokyo)]][[en:Ōji Shrine]][[ja:王子神社 (東京都北区)]][[pl:Ōji-jinja]][[zh:王子神社 (東京都北區)]] [[co:commons:Category:Oji jinja (Tokyo)]][[en:Ōji Shrine]][[ja:王子神社 (東京都北区)]][[pl:Ōji-jinja]][[zh:王子神社 (東京都北區)]] [[en:Ōji Shrine]][[ja:王子神社 (東京都北区)]][[pl:Ōji-jinja]][[zh:王子神社 (東京都北區)]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in the Kita-ku ward of Tokyo, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ōji Shrine
王子神社 | image = File:Oji Shrine 01.JPG | caption = The ''[[honden]]'' of Oji shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.75327778|N|139.73591667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}}; {{ill|Izanagi and Izanami|WD=Q12259961|1=Izanagi and Izanami}} | founded_by = | established = 1320s | date_destroyed = | location = 1 Chome-1-12 Ōjihonchō, {{ill|Kita, Tokyo|en|Kita, Tokyo|WD=Q235130|lt=Kita-ku|1=Kita, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}} [[:en:〒|〒]] 114-0022 | website = {{URL|http://ojijinja.tokyo.jp}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|WD=Q3155268|1=Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} | festival = | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Ōji-Honchō|WD=Q11572988|1=Ōji-Honchō}} | part_of= {{ill|Tokyo-jissha|WD=Q164895|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines}} }} {{nihongo|'''Ōji Shrine'''|王子神社|Ōji-jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in the {{ill|Kita, Tokyo|en|Kita, Tokyo|WD=Q235130|lt=Kita ward|1=Kita, Tokyo}} of {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Established during the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, most likely around 1321–1324, the shrine gives the name of "Ōji" to this area of the city. Most of the original buildings in the shrine precincts were destroyed during {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, and were rebuilt from the late 50s to 1982. It is notable for its giant {{ill|Ginkgo biloba|en|Ginkgo biloba|WD=Q43284|lt=ginkgo tree|1=Ginkgo biloba}},{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} suspected to be 600 years old and designated a {{ill|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|en|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|WD=Q20982902|lt=Natural Monument|1=List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments}} in 1939, and for its annual festival (held in August), which includes a [[mikoshi]] parade and a performance of {{ill|Dengaku|en|Dengaku|WD=Q3022455|lt=Dengaku|1=Dengaku}} dance.{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} It is one of the {{Nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}},{{cite web |url=http://tencoo.fc2web.com/jinja/xzyussha.htm |title=Tokyo Ten Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher= |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} of which it has been designated as the "northern protector shrine".{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == History == The exact date in which the Ōji Shrine was founded is not known, but according to a stone with inscriptions (designated a [[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)|Tangible Cultural Property]] of Tokyo) still standing beside the ginkgo tree, near the shrine precincts, Toyoshima contributed to found the shrine between 1321 and 1324.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The giant ginkgo tree is suspected to have been planted at around this time. {{ill|Ōta Dōkan|en|Ōta Dōkan|WD=Q386144|lt=Ōta Dōkan|1=Ōta Dōkan}} (1432-1486), the {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} and [[:en:Buddhist monk|Buddhist monk]] who designed and built the {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle|1=Edo Castle}} (now the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]]), is said to have taken shelter from a storm under a big {{ill|Castanopsis|en|Castanopsis|WD=Q1065400|lt=Castanopsis|1=Castanopsis}} tree in the shrine precincts which was destroyed, probably during {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The area of the {{ill|Kita, Tokyo|en|Kita, Tokyo|WD=Q235130|lt=Kita-ku|1=Kita, Tokyo}} {{ill|Special wards of Tokyo|en|Special wards of Tokyo|WD=Q308891|lt=ward|1=Special wards of Tokyo}} surrounding it receives the name of "Ōji" from the shrine.{{cite web |url=http://dennisamith.com/2017/11/15/the-one-about-oji-jinja-shrine-in-kita/ |title=The One about Oji Jinja Shrine in Kita |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Dennis A. Amith |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == Architecture == Most of the original buildings of Ōji Shrine were destroyed in 1945 during {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, and were rebuilt in 1959-1964[[:ja:王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社 (東京都北区)]]{{Circular reference|date=August 2023}}{{Circular reference|date=November 2020}} and in 1982.{{cite web |url=http://dennisamith.com/2017/11/15/the-one-about-oji-jinja-shrine-in-kita/ |title=The One about Oji Jinja Shrine in Kita |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Dennis A. Amith |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} [[File:Oji jinja - Torii.jpg|thumb|left|''myōjin torii'' at the main entrance]] [[File:Oji jinja - Chōzuya.jpg|thumb|right|''Chōzuya'' at Ōji shrine]] [[File:Oji jinja - Honden 3.jpg|thumb|right|Interior of the ''Honden'']] [[File:Oji jinja - Seki Jinja 1.jpg|thumb|left|''Seki Jinja'' subsidiary shrine]] [[File:Oji jinja - Ginkgo tree 3.jpg|thumb|right|The giant ''ginkgo'' tree]] === Torii and Chōzuya === The shrine entrance is marked by a big concrete {{nihongo|''torii''|鳥居||literally bird abode}} in the [[Torii#Myōjin family|''myōjin'']] style, very common in {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=Shinto architecture|1=Shinto architecture}}, characterized by curved upper lintels. A short {{nihongo|''sandō''|参道||visiting path}} leads from the torii to the honden. On its right side stand a {{nihongo|''chōzuya''|手水舎|}}, the traditional water ablution pavilion used by worshippers to purify themselves before approaching the shrine. There is a back access to the shrine through a long stairway from the Otonashi Shinsui Park. This is where the giant ginkgo tree stands. === Honden === The main building of Ōji shrine is a {{nihongo|''[[honden]]''| 本殿|main hall}} in the ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]'' style, a complex {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, the ''[[heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'', or offertory hall, and the ''honden'', are all interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on December 21, 2017 The [[kami]] worshipped here are {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}} and [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]], considered to be "especially benevolent regarding marriage ceremonies, marital harmony, and restoring luck".{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} === Seki Shrine === At the left of the ''sandō'' stands a subsidiary shrine called ''Seki shrine''. This small shrine has been described as "rare even within Japan since it is dedicated to an ancestral deity in charge of hair."{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} === Ginkgo tree === The ''ginkgo biloba'' or ginkgo tree stands on a hill of the left bank of the Otonashi river, just beside the shrine precincts, at the right side of the sandō. It has a height of 24.2 m and a trunk circumference of 5.2 m. It is suspected to have been planted during the construction of the shrine, which would make it more than 600 years old.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} Unlike most of the Ōji shrine, the ginkgo tree survived the {{ill|Bombing of Tokyo|en|Bombing of Tokyo|WD=Q59886|lt=Bombing of Tokyo|1=Bombing of Tokyo}} during {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, although the top part of the trunk was damaged.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} One of the main attractions of the shrine, it was designated a {{ill|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|en|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|WD=Q20982902|lt=Natural Monument|1=List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments}} in 1939.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} Another giant ginkgo tree of similar characteristics in Tokyo is located in the grounds of {{ill|Shiba Tōshō-gū|en|Shiba Tōshō-gū|WD=Q11614668|lt=Shiba Tōshō-gū|1=Shiba Tōshō-gū}}. == Annual Events == The annual festival of Ōji Shrine is held the first Sunday of August.{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} It features a [[mikoshi]] parade, and the performance of the ''Ōji Jinja Dengakumai'' dance, one of Japan's three great {{ill|Dengaku|en|Dengaku|WD=Q3022455|lt=Dengaku|1=Dengaku}} dances.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == Access == There is no admission fee for visitors to enter the shrine precincts, and there is parking space available.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The entrances is at a 3-minute walk from the north exit of {{ill|Ōji Station (Tokyo)|en|Ōji Station (Tokyo)|WD=Q801689|lt=Ōji Station|1=Ōji Station (Tokyo)}} on the [[:en:Keihin-Tōhoku Line|Keihin-Tōhoku Line]], or from exit 3 of the same station on the {{ill|Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|en|Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|WD=Q520772|lt=Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|1=Tokyo Metro Namboku Line}}, at a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Ōji-ekimae Station|en|Ōji-ekimae Station|WD=Q16564572|lt=Ōji-ekimae Station|1=Ōji-ekimae Station}} and a 7-minute walk from {{ill|Asukayama Station|en|Asukayama Station|WD=Q4811868|lt=Asukayama Station|1=Asukayama Station}} on the {{ill|Toden Arakawa Line|en|Toden Arakawa Line|WD=Q1064795|lt=Toden Arakawa Line|1=Toden Arakawa Line}}.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == References == {{reflist|2}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Oji jinja (Tokyo)|Oji Shrine}} *[http://ojijinja.tokyo.jp Official website] (in Japanese) {{Kita, Tokyo}} ‎9]] ‎9]] ‎]]{{Kumano Faith}} [[en:Ōji Shrine]] {{Commons category|Oji jinja (Tokyo)}} [[ja:王子神社 (東京都北区)]] [[pl:Ōji-jinja]] [[zh:王子神社 (東京都北區)]] {{wikidata link|Q11573004}} [[Category:Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Tokyo Ten Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Ōji]]

Established during the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, most likely around 1321–1324, the shrine gives the name of "Ōji" to this area of the city. Most of the original buildings in the shrine precincts were destroyed during {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, and were rebuilt from the late 50s to 1982. It is notable for its giant {{ill|Ginkgo biloba|en|Ginkgo biloba|WD=Q43284|lt=ginkgo tree|1=Ginkgo biloba}},{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} suspected to be 600 years old and designated a {{ill|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|en|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|WD=Q20982902|lt=Natural Monument|1=List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments}} in 1939, and for its annual festival (held in August), which includes a [[mikoshi]] parade and a performance of {{ill|Dengaku|en|Dengaku|WD=Q3022455|lt=Dengaku|1=Dengaku}} dance.{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}}{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} It is one of the {{Nihongo|Tokyo Ten Shrines|東京十社|Tokyo Jissha}},{{cite web |url=http://tencoo.fc2web.com/jinja/xzyussha.htm |title=Tokyo Ten Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher= |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} of which it has been designated as the "northern protector shrine".{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == History == The exact date in which the Ōji Shrine was founded is not known, but according to a stone with inscriptions (designated a [[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)|Tangible Cultural Property]] of Tokyo) still standing beside the ginkgo tree, near the shrine precincts, Toyoshima contributed to found the shrine between 1321 and 1324.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The giant ginkgo tree is suspected to have been planted at around this time. {{ill|Ōta Dōkan|en|Ōta Dōkan|WD=Q386144|lt=Ōta Dōkan|1=Ōta Dōkan}} (1432-1486), the {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} and [[:en:Buddhist monk|Buddhist monk]] who designed and built the {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle|1=Edo Castle}} (now the [[Tokyo Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]]), is said to have taken shelter from a storm under a big {{ill|Castanopsis|en|Castanopsis|WD=Q1065400|lt=Castanopsis|1=Castanopsis}} tree in the shrine precincts which was destroyed, probably during {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The area of the {{ill|Kita, Tokyo|en|Kita, Tokyo|WD=Q235130|lt=Kita-ku|1=Kita, Tokyo}} {{ill|Special wards of Tokyo|en|Special wards of Tokyo|WD=Q308891|lt=ward|1=Special wards of Tokyo}} surrounding it receives the name of "Ōji" from the shrine.{{cite web |url=http://dennisamith.com/2017/11/15/the-one-about-oji-jinja-shrine-in-kita/ |title=The One about Oji Jinja Shrine in Kita |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Dennis A. Amith |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == Architecture == Most of the original buildings of Ōji Shrine were destroyed in 1945 during {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, and were rebuilt in 1959-1964[[:ja:王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社 (東京都北区)]]{{Circular reference|date=August 2023}}{{Circular reference|date=November 2020}} and in 1982.{{cite web |url=http://dennisamith.com/2017/11/15/the-one-about-oji-jinja-shrine-in-kita/ |title=The One about Oji Jinja Shrine in Kita |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Dennis A. Amith |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} [[File:Oji jinja - Torii.jpg|thumb|left|''myōjin torii'' at the main entrance]] [[File:Oji jinja - Chōzuya.jpg|thumb|right|''Chōzuya'' at Ōji shrine]] [[File:Oji jinja - Honden 3.jpg|thumb|right|Interior of the ''Honden'']] [[File:Oji jinja - Seki Jinja 1.jpg|thumb|left|''Seki Jinja'' subsidiary shrine]] [[File:Oji jinja - Ginkgo tree 3.jpg|thumb|right|The giant ''ginkgo'' tree]] === Torii and Chōzuya === The shrine entrance is marked by a big concrete {{nihongo|''torii''|鳥居||literally bird abode}} in the [[Torii#Myōjin family|''myōjin'']] style, very common in {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=Shinto architecture|1=Shinto architecture}}, characterized by curved upper lintels. A short {{nihongo|''sandō''|参道||visiting path}} leads from the torii to the honden. On its right side stand a {{nihongo|''chōzuya''|手水舎|}}, the traditional water ablution pavilion used by worshippers to purify themselves before approaching the shrine. There is a back access to the shrine through a long stairway from the Otonashi Shinsui Park. This is where the giant ginkgo tree stands. === Honden === The main building of Ōji shrine is a {{nihongo|''[[honden]]''| 本殿|main hall}} in the ''[[Ishi-no-ma-zukuri]]'' style, a complex {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} structure in which the ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or worship hall, the ''[[heiden (Shinto)|heiden]]'', or offertory hall, and the ''honden'', are all interconnected under the same roof in the shape of an H.Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen-zukuri ''Gongen-zukuri''] accessed on December 21, 2017 The [[kami]] worshipped here are {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}} and [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]], considered to be "especially benevolent regarding marriage ceremonies, marital harmony, and restoring luck".{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} === Seki Shrine === At the left of the ''sandō'' stands a subsidiary shrine called ''Seki shrine''. This small shrine has been described as "rare even within Japan since it is dedicated to an ancestral deity in charge of hair."{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} === Ginkgo tree === The ''ginkgo biloba'' or ginkgo tree stands on a hill of the left bank of the Otonashi river, just beside the shrine precincts, at the right side of the sandō. It has a height of 24.2 m and a trunk circumference of 5.2 m. It is suspected to have been planted during the construction of the shrine, which would make it more than 600 years old.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} Unlike most of the Ōji shrine, the ginkgo tree survived the {{ill|Bombing of Tokyo|en|Bombing of Tokyo|WD=Q59886|lt=Bombing of Tokyo|1=Bombing of Tokyo}} during {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, although the top part of the trunk was damaged.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} One of the main attractions of the shrine, it was designated a {{ill|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|en|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|WD=Q20982902|lt=Natural Monument|1=List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments}} in 1939.{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyobiketrip.com/column/to-make-tokyo-ctcling-more-fun-oji-jinja-no-icho.html |title=Oji Jinja no Icho |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Miracle Cyclic Tour |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} Another giant ginkgo tree of similar characteristics in Tokyo is located in the grounds of {{ill|Shiba Tōshō-gū|en|Shiba Tōshō-gū|WD=Q11614668|lt=Shiba Tōshō-gū|1=Shiba Tōshō-gū}}. == Annual Events == The annual festival of Ōji Shrine is held the first Sunday of August.{{cite web |url=https://tokyo.digi-joho.com/attractions/oji-attractions.html |title=Oji, a greenery historic town with budget-friendly attractions |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Tokyo Travel |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} It features a [[mikoshi]] parade, and the performance of the ''Ōji Jinja Dengakumai'' dance, one of Japan's three great {{ill|Dengaku|en|Dengaku|WD=Q3022455|lt=Dengaku|1=Dengaku}} dances.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == Access == There is no admission fee for visitors to enter the shrine precincts, and there is parking space available.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} The entrances is at a 3-minute walk from the north exit of {{ill|Ōji Station (Tokyo)|en|Ōji Station (Tokyo)|WD=Q801689|lt=Ōji Station|1=Ōji Station (Tokyo)}} on the [[:en:Keihin-Tōhoku Line|Keihin-Tōhoku Line]], or from exit 3 of the same station on the {{ill|Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|en|Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|WD=Q520772|lt=Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|1=Tokyo Metro Namboku Line}}, at a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Ōji-ekimae Station|en|Ōji-ekimae Station|WD=Q16564572|lt=Ōji-ekimae Station|1=Ōji-ekimae Station}} and a 7-minute walk from {{ill|Asukayama Station|en|Asukayama Station|WD=Q4811868|lt=Asukayama Station|1=Asukayama Station}} on the {{ill|Toden Arakawa Line|en|Toden Arakawa Line|WD=Q1064795|lt=Toden Arakawa Line|1=Toden Arakawa Line}}.{{cite web |url=http://www.gotokyo.org/en/kanko/kita/spot/40686.html |title=Oji Jinja Shrine |author= |date= |website= |publisher=Official Tokyo Travel Guide |access-date=2017-12-21 |quote=}} == References ==
